Missing-data treatment: Conservative interpolation or justified imputation where required
Variables included: Health expenditure burden, population aged 65+, dependency ratio, GDP per capita, year, economy group
Robustness purpose: Provides the reference specification for the main econometric and machine-learning models
Main result/interpretation: Benchmark results show that demographic aging and macroeconomic capacity are dominant predictors of health expenditure burden

Variables included: Same model structure as R1 using non-imputed observations
Robustness purpose: Tests whether the findings depend on imputed values
Main result/interpretation: Direction of key effects remains broadly consistent, supporting the stability of the main findings

Missing-data treatment: Uses only variables with strong data coverage
Variables included: Health expenditure burden, population aged 65+, dependency ratio, GDP per capita
Robustness purpose: Tests whether results remain stable without high-missingness financing and workforce variables
Main result/interpretation: Core demographic and macroeconomic variables continue to explain major variation in health expenditure burden

Missing-data treatment: Adds government financing share where available or conservatively imputed
Variables included: Core variables + government financing share + public financing exposure
Robustness purpose: Tests whether financing structure changes model interpretation
Main result/interpretation: Public financing variables improve policy interpretation but are treated cautiously because of limited country-year coverage

Missing-data treatment: Adds long-term care workforce indicators where available
Variables included: Core variables + long-term care workforce + workforce adequacy ratio
Robustness purpose: Tests whether workforce capacity improves explanatory or predictive interpretation
Main result/interpretation: Workforce variables provide useful service-capacity insight, but limited data coverage restricts generalization

Robustness purpose: Tests whether results are driven by mature health and long-term care systems
Main result/interpretation: High-income economies show higher reported health expenditure burden and stronger aging-related fiscal exposure

Robustness purpose: Tests whether effects differ in less mature long-term care systems
Main result/interpretation: Emerging economies show lower current reported expenditure pressure but potential future risk as aging and formal care demand increase

Variables included: Public financing exposure, Fiscal Pressure Index, affordability ratio, workforce adequacy ratio
Robustness purpose: Tests whether conclusions depend on the selected sustainability indicator
Main result/interpretation: Main conclusions remain directionally consistent across alternative aging-related financing sustainability indicators
